retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Technique or Treatment -- Vaginal Delivery
retrieved_docs.content: Now, attention is turned to the second infant. An ultrasound is performed to assess the position of the second twin. If, on ultrasound, Twin B is found to be in the cephalic position, with the fetal head as the presenting part, the plan is to continue the labor process with artificial rupture of membranes of the second amniotic sac. [31] If twin B is engaged in the maternal pelvis, delivery of the second infant proceeds in the same fashion as the first. If the ultrasound demonstrates that the second twin is in the breech or transverse position, maneuvers can be performed to proceed with breech delivery. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Fetal Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications -- Specialized Third Trimester Ultrasound Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Third-trimester ultrasounds are indication-driven examinations. Examples of types of specialized ultrasounds include the biophysical profile, additional biometric measurements follow-ups (every 3 to 4 weeks), doppler ultrasounds in the setting of growth restrictions. Indications include fetal growth restriction or suspected macrosomia, multifetal gestation, twin-twin transfusion syndrome screening (every two weeks), a significant discrepancy between the uterine size and clinical dates, evaluation of fetal well-being, evaluation of prelabour rupture of membranes, preterm labor, vaginal bleeding, suspected placental abruption, follow-up evaluation for placental appearance for suspected placenta previa, vasa previa, PAS, suspected fetal death, suspected amniotic fluid abnormalities. [1] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second- and Third-Trimester Imaging Studies and Antenatal Fetal Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, in patients who are identified as having high-risk pregnancies or clinical suspicion of a pregnancy complication (eg, fundal height less than gestational age or vaginal bleeding), ultrasound imaging may be performed for diagnostic evaluation or as part of antenatal surveillance. Indications for additional ultrasound evaluation in the second or third trimesters include a vast array of issues that necessitate assessment of fetal growth and well-being, amniotic fluid levels, and/or placental location and bleeding. Typically, fetal growth assessments require serial ultrasounds performed at 4-week intervals. [19] See StatPearls' companion references, " Fetal Growth Restriction " and " Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Presentation, on the other hand, refers to the fetal anatomic part closer to the cervix. The cephalic position is the most commonly found with the fetal head as the presenting part, and it correlates with improved delivery outcomes. Malpresentation, including breech, footling, or kneeling, is often associated with increased labor difficulty and, consequently, with increased morbidity and mortality. Ultrasound can determine both complete and incomplete variations of presentation. Both fetal lie and presentation must be assessed concurrently since an incomplete examination can misinterpret the findings. If the fetal head is closer to the cervix or lower segment of the uterus, the fetal lie in the sagittal axis should be confirmed before reporting a cephalic presentation since the fetus could be in a transverse axis. If a malpresentation is identified, the ultrasonographer should assess and evaluate potential causes, including placental abnormalities or fetal malformations. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multifetal gestations are associated with higher risks than their singleton counterparts. In 2021, twin births accounted for 21.3 per 1000 live births; triplet and higher-order births were 80 per 100,000 live births. [1] The recommended mode of delivery for twin gestations has been debated in the literature. Current American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology (ACOG) guidelines state that twin gestation, in general, is not an indication for a cesarean section. [2] There has been further debate regarding what types of twin pregnancies are candidates for vaginal delivery given the risks associated with a change in fetal lie after delivery of the first twin, risk of placental abruption due to the abrupt decompression of the uterus after delivery of the first twin, cord prolapse, and changes in cervical dilation that may hinder the delivery of the second twin. [3] The Twin Birth Study, the first large randomized controlled trial evaluating twin birth outcomes, found there was no increased risk of neonatal morbidity or mortality in patients who underwent vaginal delivery versus cesarean section. [4] However, the management of twin delivery is challenging for obstetricians due to issues associated with monitoring both twins during labor and the maneuvers that may be necessary to deliver the second twin.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating the fetal lie is of paramount importance, especially as the term date approaches. First, the fetal head is identified, followed by the direction of the hyperechoic fetal spine. If the fetal head is closer to the cervix, this is described as cephalad. The laterality of the spine is also essential, and it is always described based on the mother's orientation. On the other hand, when the head is more cephalad to the mother, it is considered a breech presentation. The specific type of breech presentation should be evaluated as frank, complete, or footling. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Breech Presentation -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for the breech presentation include the following: Face and brow presentation Fetal anomalies Fetal death Grand multiparity Multiple pregnancies Oligohydramnios Pelvis Anatomy Preterm labor Primigravida Uterine anomalies

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetric ultrasound evaluations for twin gestations depend on determining the chorionicity of the gestation. An ultrasound at 16 weeks for a fetal anatomy survey is appropriate in dizygotic twin gestations. Subsequently, it should be done every 3 to 4 weeks for fetal growth and confirmation of fetal anatomy. [16] Multiple fetal anatomy evaluations minimize the risk of missing a fetal anomaly and also allow diagnosis of abnormalities that may not present or be available for early diagnosis. Dizygotic twins have an additive risk for congenital anomalies. In monozygotic twins, there is an exaggerated risk for midline anomalies and cardiac anomalies that merit routine fetal echocardiography at 22 weeks. All twin gestations are also at an increased risk for abnormal placentation (placenta previa) and velamentous cord insertions with its attendant risk for vasa previa. Transvaginal scan at 18 weeks with color Doppler mapping allows detection of vasa previa. Intrapartum rupture of the second sac also entails the risk of rupturing an unappreciated gestational vessel; thus, the wisdom for seeking recognition of any pulsation in the membranes before their rupture. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Introduction -- Third Stage Abnormality
retrieved_docs.content: Placental retention duration of more than 30 minutes following fetal delivery

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 1st Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant women will typically have at least one ultrasound during their pregnancies. If only one ultrasound can be performed during pregnancy, it should be performed in the mid-second trimester (18 to 22 weeks) to detect fetal anomalies and growth abnormalities. [3] [4] First trimester ultrasounds are not a requirement for all pregnancies to confirm viability. [5] However, if any high-risk features (advanced maternal age, twin pregnancy, etc.) or clinical concerns are present (irregular menses, vaginal bleeding, etc.), more than one ultrasound may be performed at the clinician's discretion, with the first ideally in the late first trimester between 10+0 and 13+6 weeks. [5] However, ultrasound exams before ten weeks are common and can still be used as accurate assessments for most necessary clinical determinations. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Operative vaginal delivery may be required to complete the delivery of the second twin. Indications for an operative vaginal delivery are a prolonged second stage of labor, suspicion of fetal compromise, or maternal benefit. [43] A patient is a candidate for an operative vaginal delivery given an assessment of the following: the cervix is fully dilated, membranes have been ruptured, the bladder has been emptied, an estimated fetal weight is reviewed, the maternal pelvis is deemed adequate, fetal station and fetal head position are evaluated, and adequate anesthesia is confirmed. [43] If the fetal head is not engaged in the pelvis or if the head position cannot be determined, an operative vaginal delivery is contraindicated. [43] Operative vaginal delivery is performed with either forceps or a vacuum device. There are different forceps devices used based on the specific circumstance; these include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antepartum care, or prenatal care, is the health care provided during pregnancy to optimize outcomes for both the mother and the fetus. The primary objectives are to identify high-risk pregnancies and monitor the health of the mother and the development of the fetus. Following an initial visit that ideally occurs in the first trimester, regular follow-up visits are scheduled every 4 weeks in the second trimester (ie, from 14 to 28 weeks gestation) and more frequently in the third trimester (ie, from 28 weeks to delivery), increasing to every 2 weeks from 28 to 36 weeks gestation, then every week from 36 weeks until birth. [1] High-risk pregnancies necessitate more frequent surveillance. Antepartum care in the second and third trimester monitors a wide array of issues during the pregnancy course, involving ongoing clinical assessment, conducting various laboratory and imaging studies, patient counseling, and preparation for delivery. Prenatal care has become the most frequently utilized healthcare service within the United States (US), with 98% of women who give birth initiating regular care at some point in their pregnancy. [2] After the first positive pregnancy test, care is typically sought by patients and begins after a confirmed sonographic intrauterine pregnancy. In the US, the average number of visits ranges between 12 and 14, depending on the complexity of the pregnancy course. However, the World Health Organization (WHO) has recommended a minimum of 8 visits for low-risk patients. [1]

Ultrasound should be performed to confirm there is no funic presentation or other malpresentation that would necessitate a cesarean section. There is no time limit between the delivery of the infants, so the decision to proceed with cesarean section is provider-dependent. [40]

retrieved_docs.content: Head Circumference:  Head circumference is accurate within 7 to 12 days, and in the second trimester may be the most accurate. The circumference is obtained in the same plane as the Biparietal Diameter, measuring the circumference of the skull. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An important aspect of the examination in the third trimester is the evaluation of the amniotic fluid. There remains some controversy about the best methodology when there are moderate abnormalities in fluid volume, but more concordance exists at the extremes. The initial evaluation should subjectively allow for assessment for polyhydramnios and oligohydramnios. It is important to remember that near-term, amniotic fluid volume may appear decreased due to the relatively larger size of the fetus. Objective measurements should follow either using the deepest vertical pocket method (DVP) or amniotic fluid index (AFI). A diagnosis of oligohydramnios should prompt emergent referral and assessment by an obstetrician due to the associated prognosis of fetal demise. On the other hand, polyhydramnios requires a less emergent consultation but should prompt further evaluation and testing since it can be associated with complications for the mother and the fetus. In the case of multiple gestations, an increased amniotic fluid volume must raise immediate suspicion for an etiology related to a fetal abnormality. The majority of cases are related to twin to twin transfusion syndrome. [27]

retrieved_docs.content: Specifically, following 1 breech delivery, the recurrence rate for the second pregnancy was nearly 10%, and for a subsequent third pregnancy, it was 27%. Some have also described prior cesarean delivery as increasing the incidence of breech presentation twofold.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: The category “MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY” includes a range of specific child conditions that can markedly influence the course of childbirth. Among these are breech malpresentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, septuplet pregnancy, حمل بأربعة توائم (a pregnancy with quadruplets), حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), and سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition). These entities represent complex fetal presentations and multiple‑gestation pregnancies, each requiring specialized obstetric management to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2265
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a broad spectrum of obstetric scenarios that influence how birth is managed. The category includes routine situations such as a normal pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery, as well as standard delivery procedures that are applied when labor progresses without complication. It also encompasses a variety of multiple‑gestation pregnancies—twin (حمل توأمي), triplet (حمل بثلاثة توائم), quadruplet (حمل بأربعة توائم), and even septuplet (Septuplet pregnancy) pregnancies—each presenting unique challenges for monitoring, timing of delivery, and neonatal care. Fetal malpresentation is another important element within this group; examples include poor fetal positioning (سوء تموضع الجنين) and breech presentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, illustrating how obstetric interventions can correct unfavorable fetal positions to facilitate a safer vaginal birth. Together, these conditions highlight the need for individualized assessment and tailored management strategies to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a range of obstetric issues that can influence the course and outcome of childbirth. Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation illustrates a scenario in which an abnormal fetal position is corrected before labor, facilitating a safer vaginal delivery. Polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy represents a cervical lesion that may interfere with cervical competence, increase the risk of bleeding, or complicate the progression of labor. Wanrenhouding; foetale hydrops (fetal hydrops) denotes a severe accumulation of fluid in fetal compartments, a condition that can jeopardize fetal well‑being and necessitate specialized delivery planning. حمل بثلاثة توائم (pregnancy with three twins, i.e., triplet pregnancy) introduces multiple‑fetus dynamics that heighten the risk of preterm labor, malpresentation, and maternal strain, requiring careful monitoring and often a tailored delivery approach. سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) refers to any deviation from the optimal fetal orientation for birth, such as breech or transverse lie, which can complicate labor progress and may require operative intervention. Together, these conditions underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and individualized management strategies to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2335
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric scenarios. Among them, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation where a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before delivery, improving the likelihood of a vaginal birth. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes a cervical neoplasm that can complicate gestation and influence the mode and safety of delivery. The category also encompasses حمل بأربعة توائم (a quadruple pregnancy), which presents heightened risks for both mother and fetuses and often requires specialized monitoring and delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) refers to abnormal positioning of the fetus that can impede the progress of labor. MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is related to Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery), indicating that when these conditions are appropriately managed or resolved, a normal, uncomplicated vaginal delivery may still be achievable. Understanding how each of these conditions interacts with labor and delivery is essential for optimizing maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: The “Maternal and Fetal Conditions Affecting Labor and Delivery” category encompasses a network of maternal, fetal, and placental factors that shape pregnancy, labor, and birth outcomes. Maternal risk contributors include a previous cesarean section (عملية قيصرية سابقة) with its associated uterine scar (ندبة رحمية) or uterine scar from prior uterine surgery (Uteruslitteken van vroegere operatie), cervical insufficiency, and multiple gestations such as twin (حمل توأمي), triplet (حمل بثلاثة توائم) and quadruplet pregnancies, all of which serve as parent conditions for the category. Fetal contributors are represented by a spectrum of malpresentations/malpositions, including breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation (مّجيء وَجْهي), mentum presentation, transverse or oblique presentation (المَجيء المستعرض), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic (cord) presentation (the inverse of “سوء المجيء الجنيني”), and prolapsed arm (تدلّي ذراع الجنين). These abnormal presentations

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2380
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are classified as both a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) and a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض), indicating that these obstetric problems can impede the progress of labor in either stage. Recognizing that malposition, fetal size disproportion, or other labor complications may lead to a lengthened first stage as well as a lengthened second stage underscores the need for vigilant monitoring and timely management to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a classification that includes a range of obstetric scenarios such as Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation, těhotenství s paterčaty, zwangerschap; zesling, حمل توأمي, حمل بثلاثة توائم, حمل بأربعة توائم, and سوء تموضع الجنين. This category is directly related to Normal delivery procedure and to Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (& [spontaneous vaginal delivery]), indicating that these conditions are managed within the framework of routine labor and delivery practices. Recognizing and appropriately addressing each of these conditions—whether converting a breech presentation, planning for twin, triplet, or quadruplet pregnancies, or correcting fetal malposition—supports optimal maternal and fetal outcomes during labor.

This interconnection underscores the relevance of vertex presentation deliveries in understanding and managing بضع الارتفاق within patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that affect labor and delivery comprise a broad obstetric category that includes abnormal fetal presentations, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, cervical pathology, amniotic‑membrane and fluid disorders, fetal structural anomalies, and bone‑health considerations influencing obstetric management. Abnormal fetal presentations documented in this domain are breech malpresentation (often converted to cephalic), face or brow presentation (including mentum variant), transverse or oblique presentation, shoulder (arm) presentation, funic presentation, poor fetal presentation, unstable fetal position, fetal arm prolapse, and fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion; fetal disproportion may also result from myelomeningocele. These atypical positions frequently require operative vaginal delivery, special obstetric maneuvers, or cesarean section rather than routine spontaneous vaginal birth. retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2271
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a classification that includes a range of specific obstetric situations and interventions. It encompasses breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation, cervical cerclage suture present, cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy, polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy, and previous operation to cervix affecting pregnancy. The category also covers multiple gestations such as حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), حمل بثلاثة توائم (triplet pregnancy), and حمل بأربعة توائم (quadruplet pregnancy). Each of these conditions can significantly influence the progress and management of labor and delivery, necessitating careful clinical assessment and tailored care to ensure optimal maternal and fetal outcomes.
